Name: SS Yongala Wreck
Description: One of the worlds best wreck dives, the SS Yongala sank in 1911 and is now an artificial reef teeming with marine life. The 109-meter vessel attracts massive schools of fish and pelagic species.
Marine Life: Bull sharks, eagle rays, sea snakes, giant grouper, trevally, barracuda
Hazards: Strong currents, penetration hazards, marine life encounters
Facilities: Day boat access, advanced certification required

Name: Muiron Island
Description: Beautiful coral gardens and diverse marine life around Muiron Islands, featuring excellent visibility and abundant fish species. Perfect for intermediate divers looking to explore pristine reef systems.
Marine Life: Manta rays, whale sharks, tropical fish, coral trout, reef sharks
Facilities: Boat access only, no shore facilities

Name: Hat Head Bommie
Description: Isolated reef outcrop with ledges and pelagics.

Name: Black Rock – Forster
Description: Rocky outcrop with strong currents and deep ledges.

Name: HMAS Brisbane
Description: Well-prepared wreck with easy access, great for all diver levels.

Name: Arno Bay Jetty
Description: Sheltered jetty good for training and macro life.

Name: Aldinga Drop-off
Description: Sheer underwater cliff with ledges, overhangs, and caves.

Name: American River Wharf
Description: Easy estuarine dive with macro life and grassy bottom.

Name: Ningaloo Reef
Description: Western Australias fringing coral reef offering easy shore access and pristine coral gardens. Famous for whale shark encounters and manta ray cleaning stations.
Marine Life: Whale sharks, manta rays, reef sharks, turtles, coral trout
Hazards: Strong shore surge, seasonal marine life

Name: Exmouth Navy Jetty
Description: Concrete jetty home to juvenile fish, octopus and nudibranchs.

Name: Fish Rock Cave
Description: Ocean cave and shark hotspot with tunnel dives and big marine life.

Name: Montague Island
Description: Diving at Montague Island offers an unforgettable experience with crystal-clear waters, playful Australian fur seals, and vibrant marine life.
Marine Life:
Fur seals 
Grey nurse sharks 
Reef fish including angelfish, butterflyfish, parrotfish, kingfish, and trevally
Wobbegong and Port Jackson sharks resting on the seafloor

Sea turtles 
Dolphins
Humpback whales
Little penguins 
Stingrays and eagle rays
Hazards:
Strong currents – Especially on the eastern and southern sides; can challenge inexperienced divers

Cold water temperatures – Thermoclines and upwellings can cause sudden drops in temperature

Surge and swell – Common around rocky entry/exit points; can affect visibility and diver control

Limited visibility – Variable, especially after storms or heavy rain

Marine life – Although mostly safe, close interaction with seals or large sharks requires caution

Boat traffic – Popular for tours and fishing; surface marker buoys are essential

Rough surface conditions – Wind and wave exposure can make boat entry and exit difficult
Depth – Some dive sites exceed 18 metres;
Facilities:
Guided dive tours
Gear rental available
Air fills onboard
Purpose-built dive boats
Accommodation packages nearby

Name: Julian Rocks – Cod Hole
Description: Swim-through with large cod and pelagics.

Name: Oak Park
Description: Rocky reef with gullies and swim-throughs, popular for macro photography.
Nearby Runoff: Minimal - Gunnamatta Bay is a sheltered estuary; some stormwater from Cronulla residential areas
Viz Triggers: NE swell reduces viz; clearest on calm SW wind days; post-rain turbidity from bay drains lasts 1-2 days
Viz Seasonal: Summer: 8-15m, Winter: 5-10m; best on calm mornings after settled weather; autumn most reliable

Name: Blue Holes
Description: Unique coastal reef with natural "holes" and excellent snorkeling/diving.

Name: Orford Reef Giant Kelp Forest
Description: Rare giant kelp dive with deep walls and cold-water marine life.

Name: Black Point Jetty
Description: Shallow jetty with sponge growth and juvenile fish, good for macro.

Name: Chowder Bay
Description: Sheltered harbour site with good muck diving and macro subjects.
Nearby Runoff: Minimal - protected harbour location at Mosman; small local stormwater catchment
Viz Triggers: Very protected; general harbour turbidity after heavy rain; boat mooring area creates minor sediment disturbance
Viz Seasonal: Consistent 5-8m year-round; rarely drops below 4m; summer slightly clearer than winter

Name: Poor Knights Islands
Description: New Zealands premier diving destination featuring underwater archways, caves, and dramatic wall diving. Subtropical marine life thrives in this marine reserve.
Marine Life: Blue mao mao, moray eels, stingrays, kingfish, nudibranchs
Hazards: Strong currents, cave environments, depth changes

Name: Darwin Harbour Wrecks
Description: WWII wrecks with low visibility but fascinating historical appeal and reef life.

Name: Lord Howe Island
Description: World Heritage reef and sea stack with sharks, schools, and rare fish.

Name: Terrigal Haven
Description: Terrigal Haven is the Central Coast’s quintessential training and relaxation dive site. It may lack the dramatic topography of deep offshore reefs, but it compensates with calm waters, gentle reefs, and marine diversity that never fails to surprise. Whether it’s your first descent or your fiftieth return, the Haven remains a place where every dive feels personal — a sheltered corner of the sea that invites you to slow down and simply explore.
Marine Life:
Old wives – easily recognised by their silver stripes and spiny dorsal fins

Wrasse and leatherjackets – in vivid greens and yellows around the kelp beds

Moray eels – hiding in rock crevices, especially near the headland wall

Octopus and cuttlefish – masters of camouflage among the sand and sponge gardens

Schooling fish – yellowtail, mado, and sweep forming shimmering mid-water clouds

Nudibranchs – abundant in cooler months, especially on the sponges near the reef edge

Port Jackson sharks – resting on sand patches or under ledges during the day
Facilities:
Public toilets and showers: Well maintained and located beside the car park, including outdoor cold showers for rinsing.

Rinse station: Near the boat ramp — ideal for cleaning masks, fins, and cameras.

Picnic area: Grassed foreshore with shaded tables, perfect for debriefs or lunch breaks.

Cafés and food: Several cafés and restaurants overlook the Haven, including The Cove and The Reef Restaurant. A short walk uphill leads to Terrigal’s main street with bakeries and dive-friendly coffee spots.

Dive shop: Terrigal Dive Centre
 is located on the Haven itself, offering air and nitrox fills, hire gear, courses, guided dives, and boat charters to Skillion Reef and the ex-HMAS Adelaide wreck.

Accommodation: Terrigal offers hotels, serviced apartments, and holiday rentals within a few hundred metres of the Haven.

Name: Skillion Caves
Description: Scillion Caves sits below the dramatic sandstone headland known as The Skillion at Terrigal. This site features swim-throughs, small caverns, and sponge-covered rock faces. It’s a rewarding dive for experienced divers due to depth, surge, and navigation challenges. Access is usually via boat from Terrigal Haven or as an extended shore dive for advanced divers.
Marine Life: Home to wobbegongs, blue gropers, and schooling sweep. Nudibranchs and sponges coat the cave entrances. Divers often spot Port Jackson sharks resting in the sand gutters, moray eels, and the occasional kingfish passing by. In winter, giant cuttlefish display along the reef.
Hazards: Strong surge near the cave mouths in swell. Potential entanglement if entering tight sections. Watch for boat traffic above. Only suitable in calm seas.
Facilities: Access via Terrigal Haven with parking, toilets, showers, and air fills at Terrigal Dive Centre. Café and restaurant options on the foreshore.

Name: Freshwater Reef
Description: Offshore reef with soft corals and great fish life.

Name: Weebubbie Cave
Description: Gin-clear water and massive white limestone chambers.

Name: Terrigal HMAS Adelaide (Currently Closed)
Description: The HMAS Adelaide is a purpose-sunk ex-Royal Australian Navy guided missile frigate resting upright on the sandy seabed just off Terrigal. Scuttled in 2011, the 138-metre vessel has transformed into one of Australia’s most iconic artificial reefs. Her decks, passageways and control rooms now brim with marine life, offering divers an extraordinary blend of naval history and ocean biodiversity. The wreck sits in clear, temperate water and is accessible to qualified divers under permit.
Marine Life: The wreck supports dense growths of soft coral, sponges and kelp. Large schools of yellowtail, kingfish and sweep patrol the superstructure. Look for wobbegong sharks resting on the deck, eastern blue gropers cruising the hull, and the occasional grey nurse shark gliding through the shadows. Nudibranchs and pygmy leatherjackets inhabit the smaller recesses, adding bursts of colour to the steel corridors.
Hazards: Penetration diving presents entanglement and disorientation risks, entry is restricted to trained wreck divers with reels and torches. Depth and current can vary, and a safety stop is required on ascent. Fishing lines and loose cables occasionally hang from the structure.
Facilities: Terrigal Haven offers a boat ramp, parking, dive shop air fills, toilets and showers. Charter boats run regular trips from Terrigal Haven and The Haven Boat Ramp. Permits and tokens are available through local dive centres.

Name: North Bondi Bommie
Description: Rocky bommie with good fish life and occasional pelagics.
Nearby Runoff: Same Bondi Beach stormwater as North Bondi Rocks - large urban catchment from Bondi and surrounds
Viz Triggers: Same as North Bondi Rocks - northerly swells and rain events reduce viz; bommie surge from any easterly swell
Viz Seasonal: Same as North Bondi Rocks: Summer 8-12m, Autumn best (10-15m), Winter 6-10m, Spring 5-8m

Name: The Colours
Description: Vibrant reef with sponges and soft corals, popular photography site.

Name: Long Reef Wall
Description: Steep drop-off with pelagics and occasional sharks.

Name: Henderson's Rock
Description: Renowned for its dramatic topography, featuring deep ledges, kelp-covered gutters, and substantial caverns. It is a critical habitat for the endangered Grey Nurse Shark, making it a highly sought-after location for experienced divers
Region: Moreton Island, South East Queensland, Australia
Marine Life: Grey Nurse Sharks (seasonal), Wobbegong sharks, Ornate Crayfish, Brown-banded Catsharks, Bull Rays, and diverse reef fish.
Hazards: Strong currents, surge, overhead environments (caves/caverns), and entanglement risks (thick kelp).
Facilities: None (Offshore site; accessible via charter boat from Brisbane or Gold Coast).

Name: Smith Rock
Description: Smith Rock is one of the most significant and notorious dive sites in South East Queensland. Known as a "ship-killer," this jagged granite outcrop has claimed several vessels, including the Aarhus, Marietta Dal, and St. Paul. It is a site characterized by dramatic underwater topography, including deep gutters, caves, and swim-throughs, and is often frequented by large pelagic species.
Best Time: Winter (May–August) for calmer seas and better visibility; Slack water is essential.
Marine Life: Marine Life: Home to a high concentration of painted crayfish, wobbegong sharks, and turtles. You can also spot brown-banded bamboo sharks, moray eels, and schools of yellowtail kingfish. During winter, migrating Humpback whales are frequently heard and occasionally seen nearby.
Hazards:
Strong Currents: The site is highly exposed to the East Australian Current.
Surge: Shallow sections can experience heavy surge, making it dangerous near the rock face.
Navigation: The complex cave system requires excellent buoyancy and navigation skills.
Facilities: No facilities on-site. The site is only accessible by boat via commercial operators from Brisbane or the Gold Coast.

Name: Kingscote Jetty
Description: Easy jetty dive on Kangaroo Island with sandy bottom and jetty pylons.

Name: Port Hughes Barge Wreck
Description: Sunken barge with good visibility, fish life and growth.

Name: Voodoo
Description: Located on the southern headland of Botany Bay in New South Wales, Voodoo is a premier shore dive known for its dramatic underwater topography and vibrant marine life. It is often considered one of the best dives in the Sydney region for those with the experience to handle the entry.
Marine Life: Weedy Sea Dragons, Giant Cuttlefish, Port Jackson sharks, Wobbegongs, and large schools of Yellowtail. Nudibranchs
Hazards: The entry and exit require a "giant stride" off a rock ledge and a timed exit onto a slippery rock platform. Heavy surge can make the gutters dangerous, and the site is highly exposed to southerly and easterly swell
Facilities: There is a car park nearby within the National Park, but no dedicated dive facilities or freshwater showers at the immediate site.
Nearby Runoff:
Potter Point Sewage Outfall: Located approximately 400m-600m West/North-West; releases treated effluent that can drift over the site during NE winds or southerly currents.
Boat Harbour Stormwater: Direct runoff from the industrial basin and historical sandmining ponds to the West; can introduce heavy silt and tannins after significant rainfall.
Port Hacking Estuarine Outflow: Located to the South-West; heavy rain in the Sutherland Shire sends sediment plumes north that wrap around the point.
Viz Triggers:
Negative (Rain): Visibility often drops below 5m (or as low as 2m) following heavy rain (>10mm) due to sediment from Boat Harbour.
Negative (Winds): Moderate to strong winds from any direction except the South/West can "blow out" the site. North-Easterly winds specifically push the Potter Point plume toward the reef.
Negative (Swell): Site is highly exposed; avoid if any swell other than SW to NW is greater than 0.5m.
Positive (Tide): Incoming/High tide is ideal, pushing clean ocean water from the South/East onto the reef.
Positive (Current): A North-to-South current is favourable as it pulls outfall effluent away from the dive zone.
Viz Seasonal:
Autumn (Best Baseline): Typically offers the most stable conditions and is often cited as the best time for waves and steady visibility.
Winter (Clarity Peak): June to August provides the best visibility, frequently exceeding 15m–20m due to consistent southerly swells bringing in clean oceanic water.
Spring (Worst/Variable): October and November are historically the worst for visibility (averaging poor on ~28% of dives) due to increased wind and potential early plankton blooms.

Summer (Upwelling): Generally good (average 10m–12m), but susceptible to "greener" water from localized upwelling events.

Name: Whyalla Jetty
Description: Home of the giant cuttlefish aggregation during winter months.

Name: Shelly Beach
Description: Popular Sydney shore dive with calm conditions and abundant temperate marine life.
Marine Life: Dusky whaler sharks, cuttlefish, wobbegongs, blue gropers, nudibranchs
Nearby Runoff: Manly Lagoon stormwater drain approximately 200m north; significant catchment from Manly and surrounds
Viz Triggers: Drops sharply after rain from Manly Lagoon outflow within 2-4 hours; southerly swell over 1.5m reduces viz to 2-4m; strong NE chop stirs sand in shallows
Viz Seasonal: Summer avg: 10m, Winter avg: 8m; Spring worst (plankton blooms 5m); Autumn most stable (8-12m)

Name: Cape Banks Caverns
Description: Caverns and overhangs with surge channels and deep ledges.
Nearby Runoff: Cape Banks headland - minimal direct runoff; exposed headland with little development
Viz Triggers: Exposed at southern Botany Bay entrance; SE swell reduces viz significantly; sand channels inside caverns cloud quickly with surge
Viz Seasonal: Summer: 8-15m (best on calm NE mornings); Winter: 6-10m; worst during sustained SE swell

Name: Bare Island
Description: One of Sydney's most popular shore dives with great macro life and calm conditions.
Marine Life: Seahorses, octopus, weedy sea dragons, nudibranchs
Nearby Runoff: Minor stormwater from La Perouse suburb; some Botany Bay industrial runoff during heavy rain
Viz Triggers: Strong SE wind and swell reduces viz to 3-5m; north winds bring clearest water; post-rain turbidity lasts 1-2 days
Viz Seasonal: Summer: 8-12m, Winter: 6-10m; best on north to northeast winds; worst during sustained SE weather

Name: Wedge Island Caves
Description: Cave system with dramatic walls and pelagics.

Name: Hardwicke Bay Jetty
Description: Quiet jetty dive with sponge growth and sheltered conditions.

Name: Corny Point Lagoon
Description: Lagoon dive with juvenile fish and safe, shallow water.

Name: Fairy Bower
Description: Gentle slope with reef ledges and swim-throughs, great visibility.
Nearby Runoff: Some Manly urban stormwater from the peninsula; less direct runoff than adjacent Shelly Beach
Viz Triggers: Less swell-exposed than Shelly Beach; rain events affect viz within 24-48 hours; moderate NE swell reduces viz to 4-6m
Viz Seasonal: Summer avg: 10m, Winter avg: 7m; broadly similar to Shelly Beach but slightly more sheltered

Name: South Bondi Reef
Description: Reef and boulders at Bondi's southern end with cuttlefish and rays.
Nearby Runoff: Southern end Bondi Beach stormwater drain; urban catchment from Bondi and Tamarama
Viz Triggers: SE swell stirs sand rapidly; post-storm conditions worst (viz to 1-2m); strong easterly drops viz within hours
Viz Seasonal: Autumn best (8-15m, stable); Summer variable (5-12m); Winter 5-8m; Spring poorest (plankton)

Name: Neptune Islands (Shark Cage)
Description: Famous for great white shark cage diving; pelagic zone.

Name: North Bondi Rocks
Description: Sheltered site with rocky outcrops and marine life near the rock pool.
Nearby Runoff: Bondi Beach stormwater drain approximately 600m north; large urban catchment from Bondi, Bellevue Hill and surrounds
Viz Triggers: Northerly swells churn sand; strong easterly swell drops viz to under 3m; rain events reduce viz by 50-60% for 2-3 days
Viz Seasonal: Summer: 8-12m; Autumn best (10-15m); Winter: 6-10m; Spring: 5-8m (plankton blooms)

Name: Yena Gap
Description: Remote site with dramatic drop-offs and caves, accessed by long walk.
Marine Life: Bull rays, large schools of fish
Nearby Runoff: Botany Bay industrial and stormwater runoff from Port Botany; receives catchment from Cooks River and surrounding industrial areas
Viz Triggers: Post-rain turbidity from Port Botany and Cooks River can last 3-5 days; north winds push murky port water towards site; best after a week of dry weather
Viz Seasonal: Moderate 5-10m year-round; best after extended dry periods in summer; winter can be clearer in settled conditions

Name: Wigton Reef
Description: Pristine hard coral reef with moderate current.

Name: Round Top Island
Description: Close-to-shore site with seagrass and some coral bommies.

Name: Bare Island Deep Wall
Description: Wall dive adjacent to Bare Island with sponges, soft corals, and sea dragons.
Nearby Runoff: Same as Bare Island - minor stormwater from La Perouse suburb
Viz Triggers: Same as Bare Island - SE swell and wind reduces viz; deeper wall less affected by surface conditions
Viz Seasonal: Same as Bare Island: Summer 8-12m, Winter 6-10m; deep wall often has better viz than the shallows

Name: Green Island – South West Rocks
Description: Small island reef with soft coral gardens.

Name: Latitude Rock
Description: Steep reef wall with bommies and good visibility.

Name: Bluefish Point
Description: Deep site with drop-offs and large schools of pelagic fish.
Nearby Runoff: Minimal - North Head is largely national park with little development; small urban catchment from Manly area
Viz Triggers: Exposed to NE swells; sustained NE swell over 1.5m reduces viz for 2-3 days; surge can be significant during large swells
Viz Seasonal: Summer: 8-15m (excellent on calm days); Winter: 6-10m; best in settled anticyclonic conditions with light winds

Name: Mullaway Reef
Description: Bommie reef with good hard coral growth.

Name: Woody Head Reef
Description: Rocky reef dive near campground, ideal for macro spotting.

Name: Wedding Cake Island
Description: Rocky reef near Coogee Beach with swim-throughs and diverse marine life.
Nearby Runoff: Coogee Beach stormwater drain approximately 300m north; urban catchment from Coogee
Viz Triggers: Very swell-exposed; southerly storms can drop viz to 1-2m; any swell over 1m reduces viz; cold upwellings from the south can bring very clear deep water
Viz Seasonal: Summer: 8-15m; Winter: 6-12m (upwellings can boost to 15m+); most variable site in the area - can be 2m or 20m on different days

Name: Manta Bommie
Description: A collection of rocky outcrops surrounded by a rugged rocky reef, known for manta ray encounters.
Marine Life: Manta rays, leopard sharks, shovel nose sharks, stingrays, bull rays, octopus, crayfish, nudibranchs

Name: Bushrangers Bay
Description: Sheltered bay within an aquatic reserve known for grey nurse sharks, schools of fish, and easy access.
Marine Life: Grey nurse sharks, rays, schools of bream

Name: Cod Hole
Description: Famous for its resident potato cod, this iconic dive site offers close encounters with massive grouper fish. The site features coral bommies and swim-throughs where these gentle giants congregate.
Marine Life: Potato cod, Maori wrasse, reef sharks, coral trout, giant trevally
Hazards: Strong currents, deep water, large marine life requires respectful distance

Name: Fish Rock Cave
Description: One of Australia's only true ocean swim-through caves.

Name: Magic Point
Description: Shark cave with grey nurse sharks, accessible by boat only.

Name: Rapid Bay Jetty
Description: Shore dive famous for leafy sea dragons and encrusted jetty pylons.

Name: Edithburgh Jetty
Description: Macro haven with nudibranchs, cuttlefish and leafy sea dragons under a quiet jetty.

Name: Shark Cave
Description: Cave system popular for observing grey nurse sharks.

Name: The Gutter
Description: Narrow entry channel leading to reef and kelp beds, ideal for beginners when calm.
Nearby Runoff: enc_avif
Viz Triggers: quality_auto/72b162_bf1b1430efca431daf486ac1ed237311~mv2.jpg
Viz Seasonal: false

Name: Wreck of the Alert
Description: Historic clipper wreck dive with structure covered in soft corals.

Name: Stack Island
Description: Offshore island dive with caves, swim-throughs and pelagic species.

Name: Rowley Shoals
Description: Remote pristine coral atolls with epic visibility and reef walls.

Name: Eaglehawk Neck Caves
Description: Dramatic caves and kelp forests with sea dragons and sponge life.

Name: Julian Rocks
Description: A volcanic rock formation 2.5km offshore from Byron Bay, offering diverse marine life and excellent underwater photography opportunities. Protected marine park with year-round diving.
Marine Life: Grey nurse sharks, leopard sharks, turtles, rays, tropical fish
Facilities: Multiple dive operators, shore dive possible

Name: HMAS Swan Wreck
Description: Decommissioned naval destroyer sunk as a dive site off Dunsborough.

Name: Osprey Reef
Description: Stunning Coral Sea atoll with wall diving, big pelagics, and pristine coral.
Marine Life: Sharks (grey, silvertip, hammerhead), rays, reef fish

Name: Flinders Pier
Description: Melbournes most popular shore dive featuring a pier structure that attracts unique marine life. Excellent for night diving and macro photography.
Marine Life: Weedy sea dragons, seahorses, octopus, pipefish, crabs
Hazards: Cold water, limited visibility, pier structure

Name: Portsea Pier
Description: Pier dive with prolific macro life including seahorses and nudibranchs.

Name: SS Annie M. Miller
Description: Historic trawler wreck encrusted with marine life and upright on the seabed.
Nearby Runoff: Sydney Harbour catchment; multiple stormwater inflows across the harbour after heavy rain
Viz Triggers: Heavy rain drops viz to 2-3m for 2-3 days; port traffic and vessel turbulence reduces viz in shallows; best after 5+ dry days
Viz Seasonal: Consistent 5-10m year-round; best after extended dry spells; summer slightly better (8-10m) than winter (5-8m)

Name: Five Fathom Reef
Description: Popular reef system near Perth with hard corals, reef sharks, and rays.

Name: Clarkes Reef
Description: Scenic fringing reef off Albany with bommies and swim-throughs.

Name: Kilsby Sinkhole
Description: Crystal-clear freshwater sinkhole with open-top access. Great light rays.

Name: Piccaninnie Ponds
Description: UNESCO-listed wetland with cathedral-like chasms and haloclines.

Name: Mount Hypipamee Crater
Description: Volcanic crater lake with extreme depth and restricted visibility.

Name: Shark Point
Description: Deep reef with large boulders, drop-offs, and strong marine life diversity.

Name: Tank Cave
Description: Extensive flooded limestone cave system with over 7 km of passage. Permit required.

Name: Little Bay
Description: Sheltered bay with rocky reef and easy access, ideal for training.

Name: Kurnell Monument
Description: Deep reef wall with overhangs and strong currents, lots of pelagics.

Name: Blizzard Ridge
Description: Colourful coral ridge in Ningaloo Reef's outer lagoon.

Name: Cocklebiddy Cave
Description: Longest underwater cave penetration in Australia (~6 km). Extreme logistics.

Name: Boyinaboat Reef
Description: Easy-access reef near Perth with abundant sponge and coral life.

Name: Clovelly Pool
Description: Calm, sheltered pool area with resident blue gropers and schooling fish.

Name: Clerke Reef
Description: Remote atoll reef with stunning visibility and walls.

Name: Camp Cove
Description: Sheltered site with seagrass beds and sponge gardens, ideal for night dives.
Nearby Runoff: Minor Watsons Bay urban stormwater; very small catchment, minimal impact
Viz Triggers: Very protected harbour site; general harbour turbidity after city-wide heavy rain; ferry and boat traffic creates minor turbulence
Viz Seasonal: 4-8m year-round; lowest (3-5m) after heavy rain in greater Sydney catchment; summer marginally clearer

Name: Freshwater Reef
Description: Shallow reef with boulders, overhangs and schooling fish.

Name: Ewens Ponds
Description: Three spring-fed ponds with flowing channels and surreal freshwater clarity.

Name: The Leap
Description: Drift dive along a vibrant sponge garden wall with abundant marine life.

Name: The Apartments
Description: Underwater rock formations like building blocks with tunnels and swim-throughs.

Name: South Maroubra Reef
Description: Ledges and boulders with resident fish schools and weedy habitats.

Name: Wattamolla Lagoon
Description: Unusual dive site in Royal NP with freshwater-saltwater mix.

Name: Port Noarlunga Reef
Description: Shallow reef just offshore from jetty with rich biodiversity and swim-throughs.

Name: Rapid Bay Jetty
Description: Iconic jetty dive known for leafy sea dragons, pylons covered in marine life.

Name: Edithburgh Jetty
Description: Famous muck dive with macro life, night dives reveal cuttlefish and seahorses.

Name: North Harbour Aquatic Reserve
Description: Calm reserve area with seagrass, sand flats and rocky outcrops.
Nearby Runoff: Manly Cove stormwater minor; small urban catchment from Manly township
Viz Triggers: Semi-protected; rain events reduce viz by 30-40% for 1-2 days; plankton blooms in spring significantly reduce clarity
Viz Seasonal: 5-10m; Spring worst (plankton, can drop to 3m); Summer 6-10m; Autumn/Winter 5-8m

Name: Dee Why Wide
Description: Deep offshore reef with ledges and pelagic encounters.

Name: Little Manly Cove
Description: Protected cove great for shallow training and macro life.
Nearby Runoff: Minor Manly Cove stormwater from surrounding residential streets
Viz Triggers: Protected harbour site; general harbour turbidity after heavy rain; low-energy site rarely affected by swell
Viz Seasonal: 3-6m year-round; lowest after heavy harbour catchment rain; consistently poor compared to ocean sites

Name: The Steps Drift
Description: Scenic drift between Steps and Monument, full of soft corals and life.

Name: Frenchmans Bay
Description: Sheltered sandy-bottom bay with nearby reefs, good for macro spotting.
Nearby Runoff: Minor runoff from La Perouse suburb; some Botany Bay industrial runoff impact during heavy rain
Viz Triggers: Inside Botany Bay - protected from ocean swell; affected by industrial and port runoff after rain; north winds push murky bay water in
Viz Seasonal: 5-10m; best mid-winter after dry spell; summer more variable due to boat traffic and runoff

Name: Gordon's Bay
Description: Calm bay with an underwater nature trail, perfect for relaxed diving.
Nearby Runoff: Coogee Beach stormwater drain approximately 500m north; moderate urban catchment from Coogee and Randwick
Viz Triggers: Easterly swell over 1m stirs sand and reduces viz; after rain viz drops to 3-5m for 1-2 days; SE storms push turbid water across the bay
Viz Seasonal: Autumn/Winter best: 10-15m; Summer variable (5-12m); Spring: 6-10m (plankton reduces clarity)

Name: Ewens Ponds
Description: Unique freshwater sinkhole system with crystal-clear water and aquatic plants.

Name: Port Lincoln Jetty
Description: Urban jetty with mix of macro life and sponge-covered pylons.

Name: The Bluff (Rosetta Head)
Description: Rocky headland with drop-offs, swim-throughs, and strong surge.

Name: Port Hughes Jetty
Description: Popular training site with good visibility and easy jetty access.

Name: Stony Point
Description: Access point to see leafy sea dragons in their natural habitat.

Name: Star of Greece Wreck
Description: Historic shipwreck from 1888 off Port Willunga, surrounded by reef.

Name: Port MacDonnell Breakwater
Description: Protected area with reef walls and rich southern marine life.

Name: Glenelg Dredge Wreck
Description: Historic dredge wreck near Glenelg with abundant marine growth.

Name: Second Valley
Description: Rocky reef and wall dive with caves and ledges, teeming with life.

Name: Piccaninnie Ponds
Description: World-renowned freshwater cave system with cathedral-like chambers.

Name: North Haven Wall
Description: Artificial reef wall with schooling fish and sponge growth.

Name: Port Victoria Jetty
Description: Quiet jetty dive with sponge-covered pylons and small fish schools.

Name: Noarlunga Tyre Reef
Description: Artificial reef made from tyres now hosting soft corals and fish.

Name: Port Broughton Jetty
Description: Calm jetty dive with soft corals, small fish and easy entry.

Name: Point Lowly
Description: Excellent for cuttlefish viewing, rock and kelp bottom.

Name: Wallaroo Jetty
Description: One of SA's longest jetties with abundant fish life and night diving.

Name: Port Giles Jetty
Description: Commercial jetty with deep water access and schools of fish.

Name: Ardrossan Barge
Description: Sunken barge off Ardrossan with reef growth and schooling fish.

Name: Streaky Bay Jetty
Description: West coast jetty with calm conditions and regular stingray sightings.

Name: Port Pirie Jetty
Description: Muddy-bottom jetty great for muck diving and macro life.

Name: Marion Bay Jetty
Description: Southern Yorke site with schooling fish and healthy kelp beds.
